---
title: "CS815 - Assignment 2"
author: "Jatin Preet Singh (202556602), Daniel Kashani (202568776), Hindvrat Singh Rao()"
date: "2026-04-08"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
---

# Introduction
In this study, we focus on developing a price prediction-based trading strategy for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). An LSTM model is employed to predict future price movements using historical market features, including price and technical indicators. Additionally, GA was deployed to optimise these technical inidcators for their best parameters based on fitness that aligns with our objective of profitability. Based on these predictions, a rule-based trading strategy is constructed to generate buy, sell, or hold signals.

To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, the strategy is backtested on unseen data using an initial capital of ÂŁ10,000. Performance is assessed using key financial metrics such as returns, win rate, and risk-adjusted measures. Also, the strategyâ€™s performance is compared against benchmark approaches, including a random trading strategy and a passive buy-and-hold strategy, to provide a robust evaluation of its practical utility.

This work demonstrates how machine learning can be integrated with trading logic to create automated strategies and highlights the challenges and considerations involved in applying predictive models to real-world financial decision-making.

# Packages
```{r include=FALSE}
library(quantmod)
library(TTR)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(readr)
library(GA)
library(zoo)
library(caret)
library(keras3)
library(abind)
library(patchwork)
library(gridExtra)
library(knitr)
library(scales)
```

# Inputs

We choose the asset to be SPY, as it broadly covers the US equity market behaviour. Data used was from January 2021 to January 2026, considering post Covid market stability.

```{r include=FALSE}
ticker <- "SPY"
start_date <- as.Date("2021-01-01")
end_date <- as.Date("2026-01-31")
train_ratio <- 0.80

ema_period <- 12
rsi_period <- 14

macd_fast <- 12
macd_slow <- 26
macd_sig  <- 9

```


# Downloading the data

The data was download from Yahoo Finance for the selected asset and date range. It is returned as a time series object, so I first inspect it and then convert it into a normal data frame for easier use later.

```{r include=FALSE}
getSymbols(Symbols = ticker, src = "yahoo", from = start_date, to = end_date, auto.assign = TRUE)

stock_xts <- get(ticker)
```

# Inspecting the downloaded object

```{r }
str(stock_xts)
head(stock_xts)
tail(stock_xts)
```

```{r include=FALSE}
data_raw <- data.frame(
  date = index(stock_xts),
  coredata(stock_xts)
)

colnames(data_raw) <- c(
  "date",
  "open",
  "high",
  "low",
  "close",
  "volume",
  "adjusted"
)

data_raw$date <- as.Date(data_raw$date)

data_raw <- data_raw %>%
  arrange(date)

head(data_raw)
tail(data_raw)
```

# Data preprocessing

EDA on data:

```{r include=FALSE}
dim(data_raw)
summary(data_raw)
```

# Checking missing values

check whether any values are missing in the raw data.

```{r}
colSums(is.na(data_raw))
```

# Checking duplicate dates

Since this is daily time-series data, each trading day should appear only once.so checking whether there are any duplicate dates.

```{r}
sum(duplicated(data_raw$date))
```

If any duplicate dates exist, I remove them and keep the first occurrence.

```{r}
data_raw <- data_raw %>%
  distinct(date, .keep_all = TRUE)
```

# Making sure the data is sorted by date

This step ensures that the observations remain in chronological order, which is very important for time-series analysis and backtesting.

```{r}
data_raw <- data_raw %>%
  arrange(date)
```


```{r include=FALSE}
head(data_raw)
tail(data_raw)
dim(data_raw)
summary(data_raw)
```

# Exploratory overview of the data

We create a few simple plots to understand the stock data before generating technical indicators.

# Plot of adjusted closing price

```{r echo=FALSE}
ggplot(data_raw, aes(x = date, y = adjusted)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = paste("Adjusted Closing Price of", ticker),
    x = "Date",
    y = "Adjusted Close"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Plot of trading volume

```{r echo=FALSE}
ggplot(data_raw, aes(x = date, y = volume)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkorange") +
  labs(
    title = paste("Trading Volume of", ticker),
    x = "Date",
    y = "Volume"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```


```{r include=FALSE}
data_features <- data_raw %>%
  mutate(
    simple_return = (adjusted / lag(adjusted)) - 1,
    log_return = log(adjusted / lag(adjusted))
  )
```

```{r include=FALSE}
head(data_features)
summary(data_features$simple_return)
summary(data_features$log_return)
```
#Generating TEchnical indicators
1. MACD
2. EMAs
3. RSI

```{r}
add_ema_feature <- function(df, price_col = "adjusted", period) {
  df$ema <- TTR::EMA(df[[price_col]], n = as.integer(period))
  return(df)
}

add_rsi_feature <- function(df, price_col = "adjusted", period) {
  col_name <- paste0("rsi_", period)
  df[[col_name]] <- TTR::RSI(df[[price_col]], n = as.integer(period))
  return(df)
}

add_macd_feature <- function(df, price_col = "adjusted", fast, slow, sig) {
  macd_obj <- TTR::MACD(
    x = df[[price_col]],
    nFast = as.integer(fast),
    nSlow = as.integer(slow),
    nSig = as.integer(sig),
    maType = EMA
  )

  macd_name   <- paste0("macd_", fast,"_", slow,"_", sig)
  signal_name <- paste0("signal_", fast,"_", slow,"_", sig)
  hist_name   <- paste0("hist_", fast,"_", slow,"_", sig)

  df[[macd_name]]   <- macd_obj[, 1]
  df[[signal_name]] <- macd_obj[, 2]
  df[[hist_name]]   <- macd_obj[, 1] - macd_obj[, 2]
  return(df)
}
```


```{r include=FALSE}
data_features <- add_ema_feature(
  df = data_features,
  price_col = "adjusted",
  period = ema_period
)

data_features <- add_rsi_feature(
  df = data_features,
  price_col = "adjusted",
  period = rsi_period
)

data_features <- add_macd_feature(
  df = data_features,
  price_col = "adjusted",
  fast = macd_fast,
  slow = macd_slow,
  sig = macd_sig
)
```

# Adding a few extra useful variables

adding a few simple derived variables from the price data that moight be useful for modelling.

```{r}
data_features <- data_features %>%
  mutate(
    price_change = adjusted - lag(adjusted),
    high_low_range = high - low,
    open_close_range = close - open,
    volume_millions = volume / 1000000
  )
```

```{r include=FALSE}
head(data_features)
tail(data_features)
colnames(data_features)
```


```{r include=FALSE}
colSums(is.na(data_features))
```

```{rinclude=FALSE}
data_features <- data_features %>%
  filter(complete.cases(.))
```

# Checking the cleaned feature dataset

```{r include=FALSE}
colSums(is.na(data_features))
dim(data_features)
head(data_features)
tail(data_features)
```

#GA based indicator parameter optimisationb.
Optimising MACD and RSI using GA in order to get the best parameters based on our data. The Goal is to find indicator parameters that best predict future returns.

Train-test split for GA Optimisation:
GA needs to optimised only on train data to avoid data leakage
```{r}
n_total <- nrow(data_features)
train_size <- floor(train_ratio * n_total)

train_data <- data_features[1:train_size, ]
test_data <- data_features[(train_size + 1):n_total, ]
```

```{r GA indicator optimisation}

# Combined fitness function for both MACD and RSI
fitness_combined_predictive <- function(par) {
  
  # Validate inputs
  if (length(par) < 4 || any(is.na(par)) || any(!is.finite(par))) {
    return(-1e6)
  }
  
  # Extract parameters
  macd_fast <- as.integer(round(par[1]))
  macd_slow <- as.integer(round(par[2]))
  macd_sig  <- as.integer(round(par[3]))
  rsi_n     <- as.integer(round(par[4]))
  
  # Constraints
  if (any(is.na(c(macd_fast, macd_slow, macd_sig, rsi_n)))) return(-1e6)
  if (macd_fast >= macd_slow) return(-1e6)
  if (rsi_n < 5 || rsi_n > 25) return(-1e6)
  
  # Calculate indicators
  macd_obj <- MACD(
    train_data$adjusted,
    nFast = macd_fast,
    nSlow = macd_slow,
    nSig = macd_sig,
    maType = EMA
  )
  
  rsi_vals <- RSI(train_data$adjusted, n = rsi_n)
  
  # Prepare data
  temp <- train_data %>%
    mutate(
      macd = macd_obj[, 1],
      signal = macd_obj[, 2],
      rsi = rsi_vals,
      future_return = lead(simple_return, 1)  # Next day's return
    ) %>%
    filter(complete.cases(.))
  
  if (nrow(temp) < 50) return(-1e6)  # Need enough data
  
   #PREDICTIVE POWER (50% weight)

  # MACD signal: +1 when bullish, -1 when bearish
  temp <- temp %>%
    mutate(macd_signal = ifelse(macd > signal, 1, -1))
  
  # RSI signal: +1 when oversold, -1 when overbought, 0 neutral
  temp <- temp %>%
    mutate(rsi_signal = ifelse(rsi < 30, 1, 
                        ifelse(rsi > 70, -1, 0)))
  
  # Correlation with future returns (how predictive?)
  macd_correlation <- cor(temp$macd_signal, temp$future_return, 
                          use = "complete.obs")
  rsi_correlation <- cor(temp$rsi_signal, temp$future_return, 
                         use = "complete.obs")
  
  if (is.na(macd_correlation)) macd_correlation <- 0
  if (is.na(rsi_correlation)) rsi_correlation <- 0
  
  predictive_power <- abs(macd_correlation) + abs(rsi_correlation)
  
  #SIGNAL QUALITY (30% weight)

  # Penalize noisy signals (too many crossovers/changes)
  macd_crossovers <- sum(diff(temp$macd_signal) != 0, na.rm = TRUE)
  rsi_changes <- sum(diff(temp$rsi_signal) != 0, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  # Normalize: fewer changes = better quality
  signal_quality <- 1 / (1 + (macd_crossovers + rsi_changes) / nrow(temp))
  
  # SIMPLE TRADING PERFORMANCE (20% weight)

  # Combined signal: average of MACD and RSI
  temp <- temp %>%
    mutate(
      combined_signal = (macd_signal + rsi_signal) / 2,
      position = ifelse(combined_signal > 0.3, 1, 
                 ifelse(combined_signal < -0.3, 0, NA))
    )
  
  temp$position <- zoo::na.locf(temp$position, na.rm = FALSE)
  temp$position[is.na(temp$position)] <- 0
  
  # Calculate returns with transaction costs
  temp <- temp %>%
    mutate(
      position_change = abs(c(0, diff(position))),
      transaction_cost = position_change * 0.001,  # 0.1% per trade
      strategy_return = lag(position, default = 0) * simple_return - transaction_cost
    )
  
  equity_curve <- cumprod(1 + temp$strategy_return)
  
  if (length(equity_curve) > 0 && !any(is.na(equity_curve))) {
    annual_return <- (tail(equity_curve, 1))^(252 / nrow(temp)) - 1
    volatility <- sd(temp$strategy_return, na.rm = TRUE) * sqrt(252)
    
    if (!is.na(volatility) && volatility > 0) {
      simple_sharpe <- annual_return / volatility
    } else {
      simple_sharpe <- 0
    }
  } else {
    simple_sharpe <- 0
  }
  
  # COMBINED FITNESS
  fitness_score <- (0.50 * predictive_power +# Predictive power
                   0.30 * signal_quality +# Signal quality
                   0.20 * simple_sharpe)# Trading performance
  
  return(as.numeric(fitness_score))
}

# Run combined GA
set.seed(42)

ga_combined <- ga(
  type = "real-valued",
  fitness = fitness_combined_predictive,
  lower = c(5,  20, 4,  10),   # [macd_fast, macd_slow, macd_sig, rsi_n]
  upper = c(15, 35, 12, 25),
  popSize = 50,
  maxiter = 50,
  run = 20,
  pmutation = 0.15,
  parallel = FALSE 
)

# Extract best parameters
best_params <- as.numeric(round(ga_combined@solution[1, ]))

best_result <- data.frame(
  macd_fast = best_params[1],
  macd_slow = best_params[2],
  macd_sig = best_params[3],
  rsi_period = best_params[4],
  fitness = as.numeric(ga_combined@fitnessValue),
  row.names = NULL
)

print(best_result)

# Compare with standard parameters
standard_params <- c(12, 26, 9, 14)
standard_fitness <- fitness_combined_predictive(standard_params)

cat("COMPARISON\n")
cat("Standard params (12,26,9,14) fitness:", standard_fitness, "\n")
cat("Optimized params fitness:", ga_combined@fitnessValue, "\n")
cat("Improvement:", sprintf("%.2f%%", (ga_combined@fitnessValue - standard_fitness) / abs(standard_fitness) * 100),"\n")
 
data_features <- add_rsi_feature(
  df = data_features,
  price_col = "adjusted",
  period = best_result$rsi_period
)

data_features <- add_macd_feature(
  df = data_features,
  price_col = "adjusted",
  fast = best_result$macd_fast,
  slow = best_result$macd_slow,
  sig = best_result$macd_sig
)
```


```{r include=FALSE}
colSums(is.na(data_features))

data_features <- data_features %>%
  filter(complete.cases(.))

```


```{r include=FALSE}
n_total <- nrow(data_features)
train_size <- floor(train_ratio * n_total)

train_data_optimised <- data_features[1:train_size, ]
test_data_optimised <- data_features[(train_size + 1):n_total, ]
```


```{r include=FALSE}
cat("Total rows:", n_total, "\n")
cat("Training rows:", nrow(train_data_optimised), "\n")
cat("Testing rows:", nrow(test_data_optimised), "\n")
cat("Training start date:", min(train_data_optimised$date), "\n")
cat("Training end date:", max(train_data_optimised$date), "\n")
cat("Testing start date:", min(test_data_optimised$date), "\n")
cat("Testing end date:", max(test_data_optimised$date), "\n")
```

#Preview for train and test data, along with GA optimised indicators
```{r}
head(train_data_optimised)
tail(train_data_optimised)

head(test_data_optimised)
tail(test_data_optimised)
```

#Modelling using LSTM

```{r include=FALSE}
train_raw <- train_data_optimised
test_raw  <- test_data_optimised

cat("Training rows:", nrow(train_raw), "\n")
cat("Testing rows:", nrow(test_raw))

```
# Feature selection
Some of the features seemed redundant and could negatively impact the model. We
select ones based on performs of the model

# Momentum
rsi, macd, hist
# Trends
ema spread, price_change
# Volatility
high_low_range, open_close_range
# Returns
log_return (stationary; Colah 2015 Â§3 requires stable inputs so the tanh / 
sigmoid gates aren't chronically saturated)
# Volume
volume_millions for supply/demand

```{r}
feature_cols <- c(
  "log_return",       # stationary price change
  paste0("rsi_", best_result$rsi),           # momentum oscillator [0â€“100]
  paste0("macd_", best_result$macd_fast ,"_",best_result$macd_slow,"_",best_result$macd_sig),     # trend-momentum
  paste0("hist_", best_result$macd_fast,"_",best_result$macd_slow,"_",best_result$macd_sig),     # MACD histogram  (momentum acceleration)
  "ema",            # fast trend
  "price_change",     # raw day-over-day change
  "high_low_range",   # intraday volatility
  "open_close_range", # intraday direction
  "volume_millions"   # volume context
)

n_features <- length(feature_cols)

```

print(feature_cols)

# Target: Next Day Closing Price
The target for each day is the actual closing price of the following trading day.
This turns the problem into a regression task: the model must output a continuous
price value rather than a discrete class label.

The last row of each split has no knowable next day close, so its dropped.

```{r}
make_targets <- function(df) {
  # shifts the close up by one row
  target <- c(df$close[-1], NA) 
  target
}

train_raw$target <- make_targets(train_raw)
test_raw$target  <- make_targets(test_raw)

# Drop the final row in each split
train_df <- train_raw[!is.na(train_raw$target), ]
test_df  <- test_raw [!is.na(test_raw$target),  ]

cat("Training rows:", nrow(train_df), "\n")
cat("Testing rows:", nrow(test_df), "\n")

```

# Normalisation
We scale the features to -1 to 1 using min-max scaling fitted on the training
data only, to prevent look ahead bias.

The target (next day close) is also scaled using the training close range so
that predictions can be inverted back to real prices at evaluation.

```{r include=FALSE}
# Feature scaling parameters (fitted on training data only)
scale_params <- lapply(feature_cols, function(col) {
  list(mn = min(train_df[[col]], na.rm = TRUE),
       mx = max(train_df[[col]], na.rm = TRUE))
})
names(scale_params) <- feature_cols

scale_df <- function(df) {
  out <- df
  
  for (col in feature_cols) {
    mn    <- scale_params[[col]]$mn
    mx    <- scale_params[[col]]$mx
    range <- mx - mn

    out[[col]] <- if (range == 0) {
      0
    } else {
      2 * (df[[col]] - mn) / range - 1
    }
  }
  out
}

# Target scaling parameters, fitted on training close
target_mn <- min(train_df$target, na.rm = TRUE)
target_mx <- max(train_df$target, na.rm = TRUE)
target_r  <- target_mx - target_mn

scale_target   <- function(y) 2 * (y - target_mn) / target_r - 1
unscale_target <- function(y) (y + 1) / 2 * target_r + target_mn

train_sc <- scale_df(train_df)
test_sc  <- scale_df(test_df)

train_sc$target_sc <- scale_target(train_sc$target)
test_sc$target_sc  <- scale_target(test_sc$target)
```

# Sliding-window sequences
RNNs operate over sequences (Karpathy). Lookback 10 is 10 trading days,
roughly two trading weeks, providing the LSTM with a focused short term
context to capture recent momentum shifts in RSI and EMA. Standard RNNs
fail at long term memory (Colah); the LSTM gated cell state is designed
to carry signals such as an emerging RSI crossover across the 10 day
window without vanishing. A shorter window reduces Backpropagation
Through Time (BPTT) depth, lowering the risk of vanishing gradients
while retaining the most recent and relevant price signal on daily bars.

```{r}
LOOKBACK <- 10

make_sequences <- function(df, lb) {

  # Extract feature matrix and scaled target vector
  feature_matrix <- as.matrix(df[, feature_cols])
  target_vector  <- df$target_sc

  # Number of sequences we can form given the lookback window
  n_sequences <- nrow(feature_matrix) - lb

  # Preallocate output arrays
  X <- array(0, dim = c(n_sequences, lb, n_features))
  y <- numeric(n_sequences)

  # Fill each sequence: X gets a (lb x n_features) window, y gets the next target
  for (i in seq_len(n_sequences)) {
    window_start <- i
    window_end   <- i + lb - 1

    X[i, , ] <- feature_matrix[window_start:window_end, ]
    y[i]     <- target_vector[window_end + 1]
  }

  list(X = X, y = y)
}

tr <- make_sequences(train_sc, LOOKBACK)
te <- make_sequences(test_sc,  LOOKBACK)

X_train <- tr$X;  y_train <- tr$y
X_test  <- te$X;  y_test  <- te$y

cat("Training sequences:", nrow(X_train), "\n")
cat("Testing sequences:", nrow(X_test))

```

# Validation split
We take the last 15% of the training sequences chronologically for validation.

```{r include=FALSE}
n_tr    <- nrow(X_train)
val_cut <- floor(0.85 * n_tr)

X_val   <- X_train[(val_cut + 1):n_tr, , ]
y_val   <- y_train[(val_cut + 1):n_tr]
X_train <- X_train[1:val_cut, , ]
y_train <- y_train[1:val_cut]

cat("Training sequences:", length(y_train), "\n")
cat("Testing sequences:", length(y_val))

```

# Model Architecture
Two stacked LSTM layers since deeper RNNs work better (Karpathy).

# Bidirectional wrapper
Allow the LSTM layers and processes to sequence both forwards and backwards

# First LSTM layer
64 units. Each unit maintains its own cell state (Colah), allowing the layer to
track 64 independent aspects of the sequence and extract short-range feature
interactions (RSI + volume spike). `return_sequences = TRUE` passes the full
hidden state sequence to the next LSTM layer.

# Dropout layers
Dropout 0.3 randomly zeros 30% of hidden units during training, preventing the
network from simply memorizing the training sequence.

# Second LSTM layer
32 units, forcing the network to compress its representation. This layer models
how the first layer's features evolve across the 20-day window.

# Batch normalization layer
Normalizes the compressed LSTM output before the non linear mapping in the dense
layer, helping gradients during backpropagation.
 
# Dense layer
Dense(16 unit, activation "relu") learns a non-linear mapping from the compressed
LSTM output to the prediction. ReLU mitigates the vanishing gradient problem.

# Output layer
Dense(1 unit, activation "linear") outputs a single continuous value, the scaled
next day closing price. A linear activation is used because the target is
unbounded.


```{r}
set.seed(42)

model <- keras_model_sequential(name = "lstm_priceprediction") |>
  bidirectional(
    layer_lstm(
      units            = 64,
      return_sequences = TRUE,
      name             = "lstm1"
    ),
    input_shape = c(LOOKBACK, n_features),
    name        = "bidirectional_1"
  ) |>
  layer_dropout(0.30, name = "drop1") |>
  bidirectional(
    layer_lstm(
      units            = 32,
      return_sequences = FALSE,
      name             = "lstm2"
    ),
    name = "bidirectional_2"
  ) |>
  layer_dropout(0.30, name = "drop2") |>
  layer_batch_normalization(name = "batch_norm") |>
  layer_dense(16, activation = "relu",   name = "dense1") |>
  layer_dense(1,  activation = "linear", name = "output")

summary(model)
```

# Compiling
Adam adapts the learning rate per parameter using first and second moment
estimates, which suits LSTM gate weights with very different gradient
magnitudes. Learning rate of 0.0005 prevents the forget gate from over correcting
on noisy stock data.

Mean Absolute Error (MAE) is used as the loss function because it is expressed
in the same units as the scaled price, is robust to outlier days, and gives an
intuitive reading of average prediction error.

```{r}
model |> compile(
  optimizer = optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.0005),
  loss      = "mae",
  metrics   = list("mae")
)
```

# Callbacks
WE implement early stopping because LSTMs can over fit by memorizing the 
training sequence. We monitor validation loss and stop training if it hasn't
improved for 30 consecutive epochs, then restore weights from the best epoch.
We reduce the learning rate if it platues for 5 epochs by half which
should help the optimizer escape shallow local minima on the regression loss 
surface.

```{r}
cbs <- list(
  callback_early_stopping(
    monitor              = "val_loss",
    patience             = 30,
    restore_best_weights = TRUE,
    verbose              = 1
  ),
  callback_reduce_lr_on_plateau(
    monitor  = "val_loss",
    factor   = 0.5,
    patience = 5,
    min_lr   = 0.000001,
    verbose  = 1
  )
)
```

# Training â€” Round 1 (train split)
Batch size 16 balances gradient noise and generalization. The model is trained
on the training sequences only, with the validation split used for early
stopping and learning rate scheduling.

```{r}
set.seed(42)
history <- model |> fit(
  x               = X_train,
  y               = y_train,
  epochs          = 100,
  batch_size      = 16,
  validation_data = list(X_val, y_val),
  callbacks       = cbs,
  verbose         = 1
)
```

# Training curves

```{r echo=FALSE}
hist_df <- data.frame(
  epoch   = seq_along(history$metrics$loss),
  tr_loss = history$metrics$loss,
  vl_loss = history$metrics$val_loss
)

p_loss <- ggplot(hist_df, aes(epoch)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = tr_loss, colour = "Train"),      linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(aes(y = vl_loss, colour = "Validation"), linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Train = "steelblue", Validation = "red")) +
  labs(title = "MAE Loss Round 1: Train vs Validation",
       x = "Epoch", y = "MAE scaled", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()
print(p_loss)
```

# Round 1 Test evaluation
After Round 1 training, we evaluate on the held out test sequences. Predictions
are inverted from the scaled space back to actual closing prices for
interpretation.

```{r echo=FALSE}
pred_test_sc <- as.vector(predict(model, X_test))
pred_test    <- unscale_target(pred_test_sc)
actual_test  <- unscale_target(y_test)

mae_test  <- mean(abs(pred_test - actual_test))
rmse_test <- sqrt(mean((pred_test - actual_test)^2))

cat("Round 1 Test MAE:", mae_test, "\n")
cat("Round 1 Test RMSE:", rmse_test)

# Plot predicted vs actual
test_dates <- test_sc$date[(LOOKBACK + 1):nrow(test_sc)]
plot_df <- data.frame(
  date   = test_dates,
  actual = actual_test,
  pred   = pred_test
)

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = actual, colour = "Actual"),    linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = pred,   colour = "Predicted"), linewidth = 0.7, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Actual = "steelblue", Predicted = "red")) +
  labs(title = "Round 1: Predicted vs Actual Next Day Close (Test Set)",
       x = "Date", y = "Close Price ($)", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Round 2: Retraining on Training + Validation combined
After confirming model quality in Round 1, we retrain on the full training and
validation data combined, using the optimal number of epochs found during
Round 1 (ie the epoch at which early stopping restored best weights).
This maximizes the data available before the final test evaluation, which is
good practice in time series modelling.

```{r include=FALSE}
# Combine train and validation sequences
X_trainval <- abind::abind(X_train, X_val, along = 1)
y_trainval  <- c(y_train, y_val)

# Determine the best epoch from Round 1
best_epoch <- which.min(history$metrics$val_loss)
cat("Best epoch from Round 1:", best_epoch)
```

# Reinitialise model for Round 2
We build an identical architecture and compile it fresh so that Round 2 starts
from random weights, avoiding any bias from Round 1's trained state.

```{r}
set.seed(42)
model2 <- keras_model_sequential(name = "lstm_priceprediction") |>
  bidirectional(
    layer_lstm(
      units            = 64,
      return_sequences = TRUE,
      name             = "lstm1"
    ),
    input_shape = c(LOOKBACK, n_features),
    name        = "bidirectional1"
  ) |>
  layer_dropout(0.30, name = "drop1") |>
  bidirectional(
    layer_lstm(
      units            = 32,
      return_sequences = FALSE,
      name             = "lstm2"
    ),
    name = "bidirectional2"
  ) |>
  layer_dropout(0.30, name = "drop2") |>
  layer_batch_normalization(name = "batch_norm") |>
  layer_dense(16, activation = "relu",   name = "dense1") |>
  layer_dense(1,  activation = "linear", name = "output")
model2 |> compile(
  optimizer = optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.0005),
  loss      = "mae",
  metrics   = list("mae")
)

summary(model2)
```

# Training â€” Round 2 (train + validation combined)
We train for exactly `best_epoch` epochs (no early stopping needed, as the
optimal stopping point was already determined in Round 1).

```{r}
set.seed(42)
history2 <- model2 |> fit(
  x          = X_trainval,
  y          = y_trainval,
  epochs     = best_epoch,
  batch_size = 16,
  verbose    = 1
)
```

# Round 2 Training curves

```{r echo=FALSE}
hist2_df <- data.frame(
  epoch   = seq_along(history2$metrics$loss),
  tr_loss = history2$metrics$loss
)

ggplot(hist2_df, aes(x = epoch, y = tr_loss)) +
  geom_line(colour = "steelblue", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "MAE Loss Round 2: Training + Validation Combined",
       x = "Epoch", y = "MAE (scaled)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Round 2 Test evaluation
We evaluate the Round 2 model on the same held-out test sequences and compare
performance against Round 1.

```{r echo=FALSE}
#to be used in ga
pred_test2_sc <- as.vector(predict(model2, X_test))
pred_test2    <- unscale_target(pred_test2_sc)

mae_test2  <- mean(abs(pred_test2 - actual_test))
rmse_test2 <- sqrt(mean((pred_test2 - actual_test)^2))

cat("Round 2 Test MAE:", mae_test2, "\n")
cat("Round 2 Test RMSE:", rmse_test2)

# Summary comparison
cat("\nRound one comparrison\n")

cat("Round 1 Test MAE:", mae_test, "\n")
cat("Round 1 Test RMSE:", rmse_test)
 
# Plot Round 2 predicted vs actual
plot_df2 <- data.frame(
  date   = test_dates,
  actual = actual_test,
  r1     = pred_test,
  r2     = pred_test2
)

ggplot(plot_df2, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = actual, colour = "Actual"),   linewidth = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = r1,     colour = "Round 1"),  linewidth = 0.7, linetype = "dashed") +
  # geom_line(aes(y = r2,     colour = "Round 2"),  linewidth = 0.7, linetype = "dotted") +
  geom_line(aes(y = r2,     colour = "Round 2"),  linewidth = 0.7) +

  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Actual"  = "steelblue",
                                 "Round 1" = "red",
                                 "Round 2" = "darkgreen")) +
  labs(title = "Round 1 vs Round 2: Predicted vs Actual Next Day Close",
       x = "Date", y = "Close Price ($)", colour = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()
```

# Attaching predictions to test_raw

We attach the Round 1 predictions, Round 2 predictions, and the actual next day
close back to test_raw so that the full enriched dataset is available in one
place for downstream analysis, backtesting, and reporting.

The LSTM predicts the next day close for each row in the test window. Each
predicted value corresponds to the row that is LOOKBACK + 1 steps ahead of
the first test row, so we reconstruct the matching dates from test_sc and
then left join onto test_raw

```{r include=FALSE}
# dates that correspond to each test prediction
pred_dates <- test_sc$date[(LOOKBACK + 1):nrow(test_sc)]

# Build a look up table with all three columns
pred_lookup <- data.frame(
  date          = pred_dates,
  pred_round1   = pred_test,
  pred_round2   = pred_test2,
  actual_next_close = actual_test
)

# Left join onto test_raw
test_raw <- test_raw %>%
  left_join(pred_lookup, by = "date")

head(test_raw)
tail(test_raw)
```

#Evaluation and Backtesting

1. Prediction-Based Trading Strategy:

This function executes a trading strategy based on price predictions. It compares the predicted price with the current closing price to generate buy/sell/hold signals.

```{r prediction-strategy-function}
#' Execute Trading Strategy Based on Predictions

execute_prediction_strategy <- function(data, buy_threshold = 0.02, sell_threshold = 0.02,initial_capital = 10000, transaction_cost = 0.001) {
  
  n <- nrow(data)
  
  # Initialize tracking vectors
  decision <- integer(n)          # 1=buy, -1=sell, 0=hold
  shares_held <- numeric(n)       # Number of shares held
  cash <- numeric(n)              # Cash available
  portfolio_value <- numeric(n)  # Total portfolio value
  
  # Starting conditions
  cash[1] <- initial_capital
  shares_held[1] <- 0
  portfolio_value[1] <- initial_capital
  
  # Track all transactions
  transactions <- data.frame(
    Day = integer(),
    Action = character(),
    Price = numeric(),
    Shares = numeric(),
    Cost = numeric(),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  # Iterate through each trading day
  for (i in 1:(n-1)) {
    
    if (is.na(data$Close[i]) || is.na(data$Predicted[i]) || is.na(data$Open[i+1])) {
      decision[i] <- 0
      shares_held[i+1] <- shares_held[i]
      cash[i+1] <- cash[i]
      portfolio_value[i+1] <- cash[i+1] + shares_held[i+1] * data$Close[i+1]
      next
    }
    
    # Generate trading signal
    current_price <- data$Close[i]
    predicted_price <- data$Predicted[i]
    next_open <- data$Open[i+1]
    
    signal <- 0
    
    # BUY signal: prediction significantly higher than current price
    if (predicted_price > (1 + buy_threshold) * current_price) {
      signal <- 1
    } 
    # SELL signal: prediction significantly lower than current price
    else if (predicted_price < (1 - sell_threshold) * current_price) {
      signal <- -1
    }
    
    # Execute trade (trades happen at next day's opening price)
    executed_signal <- 0
    
    # BUY: Only if we have cash and aren't fully invested
    if (signal == 1 && cash[i] > 0) {
      # Calculate how many shares we can buy
      shares_to_buy <- floor(cash[i] / (next_open * (1 + transaction_cost)))
      
      if (shares_to_buy > 0) {
        cost <- shares_to_buy * next_open * (1 + transaction_cost)
        cash[i+1] <- cash[i] - cost
        shares_held[i+1] <- shares_held[i] + shares_to_buy
        executed_signal <- 1
        
        # Record transaction
        transactions <- rbind(transactions, data.frame(
          Day = i+1,
          Action = "BUY",
          Price = next_open,
          Shares = shares_to_buy,
          Cost = cost
        ))
      } else {
        cash[i+1] <- cash[i]
        shares_held[i+1] <- shares_held[i]
      }
    }
    # SELL: Only if we have shares to sell
    else if (signal == -1 && shares_held[i] > 0) {
      # Sell all shares
      shares_to_sell <- shares_held[i]
      proceeds <- shares_to_sell * next_open * (1 - transaction_cost)
      
      cash[i+1] <- cash[i] + proceeds
      shares_held[i+1] <- 0
      executed_signal <- -1
      
      # Record transaction
      transactions <- rbind(transactions, data.frame(
        Day = i+1,
        Action = "SELL",
        Price = next_open,
        Shares = shares_to_sell,
        Cost = -proceeds
      ))
    }
    # HOLD
    else {
      cash[i+1] <- cash[i]
      shares_held[i+1] <- shares_held[i]
    }
    
    decision[i] <- executed_signal
    
    # Calculate portfolio value
    if (!is.na(data$Close[i+1])) {
      portfolio_value[i+1] <- cash[i+1] + shares_held[i+1] * data$Close[i+1]
    } else {
      portfolio_value[i+1] <- portfolio_value[i]
    }
  }
  
  # Last day: close any open positions
  if (shares_held[n-1] > 0 && !is.na(data$Close[n])) {
    proceeds <- shares_held[n-1] * data$Close[n] * (1 - transaction_cost)
    cash[n] <- cash[n-1] + proceeds
    shares_held[n] <- 0
    decision[n] <- -1
    
    transactions <- rbind(transactions, data.frame(
      Day = n,
      Action = "SELL (Close)",
      Price = data$Close[n],
      Shares = shares_held[n-1],
      Cost = -proceeds
    ))
  } else {
    cash[n] <- cash[n-1]
    shares_held[n] <- shares_held[n-1]
    decision[n] <- 0
  }
  
  portfolio_value[n] <- cash[n] + shares_held[n] * data$Close[n]
  
  # Return results
  return(list(
    decision = decision,
    shares_held = shares_held,
    cash = cash,
    portfolio_value = portfolio_value,
    transactions = transactions,
    final_value = portfolio_value[n],
    initial_capital = initial_capital
  ))
}
```

2. Buy & Hold Strategy:

A classic benchmark strategy: buy at the beginning and hold until the end.

```{r buyhold-function}
#' Buy and Hold Strategy

execute_buy_hold <- function(data, initial_capital = 10000, transaction_cost = 0.001) {
  
  n <- nrow(data)
  
  # Buy on day 1 at opening price
  first_open <- data$Open[1]
  shares_bought <- floor(initial_capital / (first_open * (1 + transaction_cost)))
  cost <- shares_bought * first_open * (1 + transaction_cost)
  remaining_cash <- initial_capital - cost
  
  # Track portfolio value over time
  portfolio_value <- numeric(n)
  shares_held <- rep(shares_bought, n)
  cash <- rep(remaining_cash, n)
  
  for (i in 1:n) {
    if (!is.na(data$Close[i])) {
      portfolio_value[i] <- remaining_cash + shares_bought * data$Close[i]
    } else {
      portfolio_value[i] <- if(i > 1) portfolio_value[i-1] else initial_capital
    }
  }
  
  # Sell on last day
  final_proceeds <- shares_bought * data$Close[n] * (1 - transaction_cost)
  final_value <- remaining_cash + final_proceeds
  
  return(list(
    shares_held = shares_held,
    cash = cash,
    portfolio_value = portfolio_value,
    final_value = final_value,
    initial_capital = initial_capital,
    transactions = data.frame(
      Day = c(1, n),
      Action = c("BUY", "SELL"),
      Price = c(first_open, data$Close[n]),
      Shares = c(shares_bought, shares_bought),
      Cost = c(-cost, final_proceeds)
    )
  ))
}
```

3. Random Trading Strategy:

A baseline strategy that makes random buy/sell decisions to validate that your strategy has genuine skill.

```{r random-strategy-function}
#' Random Trading Strategy (for comparison)

execute_random_strategy <- function(data, initial_capital = 10000, trade_probability = 0.1,transaction_cost = 0.001, seed = 42) {
  
  set.seed(seed)
  n <- nrow(data)
  
  # Initialize
  decision <- integer(n)
  shares_held <- numeric(n)
  cash <- numeric(n)
  portfolio_value <- numeric(n)
  
  cash[1] <- initial_capital
  shares_held[1] <- 0
  portfolio_value[1] <- initial_capital
  
  transactions <- data.frame(
    Day = integer(),
    Action = character(),
    Price = numeric(),
    Shares = numeric(),
    Cost = numeric(),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  for (i in 1:(n-1)) {
    
    if (is.na(data$Open[i+1]) || is.na(data$Close[i+1])) {
      decision[i] <- 0
      shares_held[i+1] <- shares_held[i]
      cash[i+1] <- cash[i]
      portfolio_value[i+1] <- portfolio_value[i]
      next
    }
    
    # Random decision
    if (runif(1) < trade_probability) {
      signal <- sample(c(-1, 1), 1)  # Randomly buy or sell
    } else {
      signal <- 0
    }
    
    next_open <- data$Open[i+1]
    
    # Execute BUY
    if (signal == 1 && cash[i] > 0) {
      shares_to_buy <- floor(cash[i] / (next_open * (1 + transaction_cost)))
      
      if (shares_to_buy > 0) {
        cost <- shares_to_buy * next_open * (1 + transaction_cost)
        cash[i+1] <- cash[i] - cost
        shares_held[i+1] <- shares_held[i] + shares_to_buy
        decision[i] <- 1
        
        transactions <- rbind(transactions, data.frame(
          Day = i+1, Action = "BUY", Price = next_open,
          Shares = shares_to_buy, Cost = cost
        ))
      } else {
        cash[i+1] <- cash[i]
        shares_held[i+1] <- shares_held[i]
      }
    }
    # Execute SELL
    else if (signal == -1 && shares_held[i] > 0) {
      shares_to_sell <- shares_held[i]
      proceeds <- shares_to_sell * next_open * (1 - transaction_cost)
      
      cash[i+1] <- cash[i] + proceeds
      shares_held[i+1] <- 0
      decision[i] <- -1
      
      transactions <- rbind(transactions, data.frame(
        Day = i+1, Action = "SELL", Price = next_open,
        Shares = shares_to_sell, Cost = -proceeds
      ))
    }
    # HOLD
    else {
      cash[i+1] <- cash[i]
      shares_held[i+1] <- shares_held[i]
      decision[i] <- 0
    }
    
    portfolio_value[i+1] <- cash[i+1] + shares_held[i+1] * data$Close[i+1]
  }
  
  # Close position on last day
  if (shares_held[n-1] > 0) {
    proceeds <- shares_held[n-1] * data$Close[n] * (1 - transaction_cost)
    cash[n] <- cash[n-1] + proceeds
    shares_held[n] <- 0
    decision[n] <- -1
  } else {
    cash[n] <- cash[n-1]
    shares_held[n] <- shares_held[n-1]
    decision[n] <- 0
  }
  
  portfolio_value[n] <- cash[n] + shares_held[n] * data$Close[n]
  
  return(list(
    decision = decision,
    shares_held = shares_held,
    cash = cash,
    portfolio_value = portfolio_value,
    transactions = transactions,
    final_value = portfolio_value[n],
    initial_capital = initial_capital
  ))
}
```

#Performance Metrics

Calculating Performance Metrics:

This function calculates performance metrics including returns, volatility, Sharpe ratio, maximum drawdown, and trading statistics.

```{r performance-metrics-function}
#' Calculate Comprehensive Performance Metrics

calculate_performance_metrics <- function(portfolio_value, 
                                         initial_capital, 
                                         transactions,
                                         risk_free_rate = 0.02) {
  
  # Total Return
  final_value <- tail(portfolio_value, 1)
  total_return <- (final_value - initial_capital) / initial_capital
  total_return_pct <- total_return * 100
  
  # Annualized Return (assuming 252 trading days per year)
  n_days <- length(portfolio_value)
  years <- n_days / 252
  annualized_return <- (final_value / initial_capital)^(1/years) - 1
  annualized_return_pct <- annualized_return * 100
  
  # Daily Returns
  daily_returns <- diff(portfolio_value) / head(portfolio_value, -1)
  daily_returns[is.infinite(daily_returns)] <- 0
  daily_returns[is.nan(daily_returns)] <- 0
  
  # Volatility (annualized)
  volatility <- sd(daily_returns, na.rm = TRUE) * sqrt(252)
  volatility_pct <- volatility * 100
  
  # Sharpe Ratio
  daily_rf <- (1 + risk_free_rate)^(1/252) - 1
  excess_returns <- daily_returns - daily_rf
  sharpe_ratio <- mean(excess_returns, na.rm = TRUE) / sd(excess_returns, na.rm = TRUE) * sqrt(252)
  
  # Maximum Drawdown
  cummax_value <- cummax(portfolio_value)
  drawdown <- (portfolio_value - cummax_value) / cummax_value
  max_drawdown <- min(drawdown, na.rm = TRUE)
  max_drawdown_pct <- max_drawdown * 100
  
  # Win Rate (for strategies with transactions)
  if (nrow(transactions) > 0) {
    # Pair buy and sell transactions
    buys <- transactions[transactions$Action %in% c("BUY"), ]
    sells <- transactions[transactions$Action %in% c("SELL", "SELL (Close)"), ]
    
    if (nrow(buys) > 0 && nrow(sells) > 0) {
      n_trades <- min(nrow(buys), nrow(sells))
      
      if (n_trades > 0) {
        profits <- numeric(n_trades)
        for (i in 1:n_trades) {
          buy_cost <- buys$Cost[i]
          sell_proceeds <- -sells$Cost[i]  # Negative because proceeds are stored as negative cost
          profits[i] <- sell_proceeds - buy_cost
        }
        
        winning_trades <- sum(profits > 0)
        win_rate <- winning_trades / n_trades
        win_rate_pct <- win_rate * 100
        avg_win <- mean(profits[profits > 0])
        avg_loss <- mean(profits[profits < 0])
        profit_factor <- ifelse(length(profits[profits < 0]) > 0,
                               sum(profits[profits > 0]) / abs(sum(profits[profits < 0])),
                               Inf)
      } else {
        win_rate_pct <- NA
        avg_win <- NA
        avg_loss <- NA
        profit_factor <- NA
        n_trades <- 0
      }
    } else {
      win_rate_pct <- NA
      avg_win <- NA
      avg_loss <- NA
      profit_factor <- NA
      n_trades <- 0
    }
  } else {
    win_rate_pct <- NA
    avg_win <- NA
    avg_loss <- NA
    profit_factor <- NA
    n_trades <- 0
  }
  
  # Return metrics as list
  return(list(
    final_value = final_value,
    total_return = total_return,
    total_return_pct = total_return_pct,
    annualized_return = annualized_return,
    annualized_return_pct = annualized_return_pct,
    volatility = volatility,
    volatility_pct = volatility_pct,
    sharpe_ratio = sharpe_ratio,
    max_drawdown = max_drawdown,
    max_drawdown_pct = max_drawdown_pct,
    win_rate_pct = win_rate_pct,
    number_of_trades = n_trades,
    avg_win = avg_win,
    avg_loss = avg_loss,
    profit_factor = profit_factor
  ))
}
```

#Comparison & Visualization Multiple Strategies

```{r compare-strategies-function, include=FALSE}
#' Compare Multiple Strategies

compare_strategies <- function(strategies_list, strategy_names) {
  
  comparison_df <- data.frame(
    Strategy = strategy_names,
    Final_Value = numeric(length(strategies_list)),
    Total_Return_Pct = numeric(length(strategies_list)),
    Annualized_Return_Pct = numeric(length(strategies_list)),
    Volatility_Pct = numeric(length(strategies_list)),
    Sharpe_Ratio = numeric(length(strategies_list)),
    Max_Drawdown_Pct = numeric(length(strategies_list)),
    Win_Rate_Pct = numeric(length(strategies_list)),
    Number_of_Trades = integer(length(strategies_list)),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
  
  for (i in 1:length(strategies_list)) {
    metrics <- strategies_list[[i]]$metrics
    comparison_df$Final_Value[i] <- metrics$final_value
    comparison_df$Total_Return_Pct[i] <- metrics$total_return_pct
    comparison_df$Annualized_Return_Pct[i] <- metrics$annualized_return_pct
    comparison_df$Volatility_Pct[i] <- metrics$volatility_pct
    comparison_df$Sharpe_Ratio[i] <- metrics$sharpe_ratio
    comparison_df$Max_Drawdown_Pct[i] <- metrics$max_drawdown_pct
    comparison_df$Win_Rate_Pct[i] <- metrics$win_rate_pct
    comparison_df$Number_of_Trades[i] <- metrics$number_of_trades
  }
  
  return(comparison_df)
}
```


```{r visualization-functions, echo=FALSE}
#' Create Visualization of Strategy Performance

plot_strategy_comparison <- function(data, strategies_list, strategy_names) {
  
  # Prepare data for plotting
  n <- nrow(data)
  
  plot_data <- data.frame(
    Day = rep(1:n, length(strategies_list)),
    Portfolio_Value = unlist(lapply(strategies_list, function(x) x$portfolio_value)),
    Strategy = rep(strategy_names, each = n)
  )
  
  # Portfolio value over time
  p1 <- ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = Day, y = Portfolio_Value, color = Strategy, linetype = Strategy)) +
    geom_line(size = 1) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = strategies_list[[1]]$initial_capital, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
    labs(
      title = "Portfolio Value Over Time",
      subtitle = paste0("Initial Capital: ÂŁ", format(strategies_list[[1]]$initial_capital, big.mark = ",")),
      x = "Trading Day",
      y = "Portfolio Value (ÂŁ)"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      legend.position = "bottom",
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10)
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma)
  
  return(p1)
}

#' Create Returns Distribution Plot
#' 
#' @param strategies_list List of strategy results
#' @param strategy_names Vector of strategy names
#' @return ggplot object
plot_returns_distribution <- function(strategies_list, strategy_names) {
  
  # Calculate daily returns for each strategy
  returns_data <- data.frame()
  
  for (i in 1:length(strategies_list)) {
    pv <- strategies_list[[i]]$portfolio_value
    returns <- diff(pv) / head(pv, -1)
    returns[is.infinite(returns)] <- 0
    returns[is.nan(returns)] <- 0
    
    temp_df <- data.frame(
      Returns = returns * 100,  # Convert to percentage
      Strategy = strategy_names[i]
    )
    returns_data <- rbind(returns_data, temp_df)
  }
  
  p2 <- ggplot(returns_data, aes(x = Returns, fill = Strategy)) +
    geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
    labs(
      title = "Daily Returns Distribution",
      x = "Daily Return (%)",
      y = "Density"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      legend.position = "bottom",
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14)
    )
  
  return(p2)
}
```

#Main Backtesting Function

This Runs the entire backtesting process, running all strategies and generating comparisons.

```{r main-backtest-function}
run_complete_backtest <- function(test_data,initial_capital = 10000,buy_threshold = 0.02,sell_threshold = 0.02,transaction_cost = 0.001, risk_free_rate = 0.02) {
  
  cat("ALGORITHMIC TRADING BACKTESTING FRAMEWORK\n")
  cat("Initial Capital: ÂŁ", format(initial_capital, big.mark = ","), "\n")
  cat("Test Period: ", nrow(test_data), " days\n")
  cat("Transaction Cost: ", transaction_cost * 100, "%\n")
  cat("Buy Threshold: ", buy_threshold * 100, "%\n")
  cat("Sell Threshold: ", sell_threshold * 100, "%\n")

  # Strategy 1: Prediction-Based Strategy
  cat("Running Prediction-Based Strategy...\n")
  pred_results <- execute_prediction_strategy(
    test_data, 
    buy_threshold, 
    sell_threshold,
    initial_capital, 
    transaction_cost
  )
  pred_metrics <- calculate_performance_metrics(
    pred_results$portfolio_value,
    pred_results$initial_capital,
    pred_results$transactions,
    risk_free_rate
  )
  pred_results$metrics <- pred_metrics
  
  # Strategy 2: Buy & Hold
  cat("Running Buy & Hold Strategy...\n")
  bh_results <- execute_buy_hold(test_data, initial_capital, transaction_cost)
  bh_metrics <- calculate_performance_metrics(
    bh_results$portfolio_value,
    bh_results$initial_capital,
    bh_results$transactions,
    risk_free_rate
  )
  bh_results$metrics <- bh_metrics
  
  # Strategy 3: Random Trading
  cat("Running Random Trading Strategy...\n")
  random_results <- execute_random_strategy(
    test_data, 
    initial_capital,
    trade_probability = 0.1,
    transaction_cost,
    seed = 42
  )
  random_metrics <- calculate_performance_metrics(
    random_results$portfolio_value,
    random_results$initial_capital,
    random_results$transactions,
    risk_free_rate
  )
  random_results$metrics <- random_metrics
  
  # Compare Strategies
  cat("Comparing Strategies\n")
  strategies_list <- list(pred_results, bh_results, random_results)
  strategy_names <- c("Prediction-Based", "Buy & Hold", "Random Trading")
  
  comparison <- compare_strategies(strategies_list, strategy_names)
  
  # Print Results
  cat("\n")
  cat("  PERFORMANCE COMPARISON\n")
  print(comparison, row.names = FALSE)
  cat("\n")
  
  # Print detailed metrics for prediction-based strategy
  cat("  DETAILED METRICS: PREDICTION-BASED STRATEGY\n")
  cat("Final Portfolio Value: ÂŁ", format(pred_metrics$final_value, big.mark = ",", digits = 2, nsmall = 2), "\n")
  cat("Total Return: ", sprintf("%.2f%%", pred_metrics$total_return_pct), "\n")
  cat("Annualized Return: ", sprintf("%.2f%%", pred_metrics$annualized_return_pct), "\n")
  cat("Volatility (Annual): ", sprintf("%.2f%%", pred_metrics$volatility_pct), "\n")
  cat("Sharpe Ratio: ", sprintf("%.3f", pred_metrics$sharpe_ratio), "\n")
  cat("Maximum Drawdown: ", sprintf("%.2f%%", pred_metrics$max_drawdown_pct), "\n")
  cat("Number of Trades: ", pred_metrics$number_of_trades, "\n")
  if (!is.na(pred_metrics$win_rate_pct)) {
    cat("Win Rate: ", sprintf("%.2f%%", pred_metrics$win_rate_pct), "\n")
    cat("Average Win: ÂŁ", format(pred_metrics$avg_win, digits = 2, nsmall = 2), "\n")
    cat("Average Loss: ÂŁ", format(pred_metrics$avg_loss, digits = 2, nsmall = 2), "\n")
    cat("Profit Factor: ", sprintf("%.3f", pred_metrics$profit_factor), "\n")
  }

  # Create visualizations
  cat("Creating visualizations...\n")
  p1 <- plot_strategy_comparison(test_data, strategies_list, strategy_names)
  p2 <- plot_returns_distribution(strategies_list, strategy_names)
  
  # Return all results
  return(list(
    prediction_strategy = pred_results,
    buy_hold_strategy = bh_results,
    random_strategy = random_results,
    comparison = comparison,
    plot_portfolio = p1,
    plot_returns = p2,
    test_data_used = test_data
  ))
}
```


```{r}
n_days <- nrow(test_raw)  
test_data <- data.frame(
  Date = test_raw$date,
  Open = test_raw$open,
  Close = test_raw$close,
  Predicted = test_raw$pred_round2
)
```


```{r display-sample-data, include=FALSE}
# Display last few rows
kable(tail(test_data, 10), 
      caption = "Sample Test Data (last 10 Days)",
      digits = 2)
```

#Running Complete Backtest

```{r run-backtest, results='markup'}
# Run backtest with default parameters
results <- run_complete_backtest(
  test_data = test_data,
  initial_capital = 10000,
  buy_threshold = 0.01,     # Buy if prediction 1% above current price
  sell_threshold = 0.01,   # Sell if prediction 1% below current price
  transaction_cost = 0.001, # 0.1% per transaction
  risk_free_rate = 0.02     # 2% annual risk-free rate
)
```

#Results & Analysis

#Performance Comparison Table

```{r comparison-table, echo=FALSE}
kable(results$comparison, 
      caption = "Strategy Performance Comparison",
      digits = 2)
```

#Portfolio Value Over Time

```{r portfolio-plot, fig.width=10, fig.height=6, echo=FALSE}
print(results$plot_portfolio)
```

Transaction Log (Prediction Strategy):

```{r transaction-log, echo=FALSE}
kable(head(results$prediction_strategy$transactions, 20), 
      caption = "Trading Transactions (First 20)",
      digits = 2)
```

# Metrics Summary

```{r metrics-summary, echo=FALSE}
# Create summary table for detailed metrics
metrics <- results$prediction_strategy$metrics

metrics_summary <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Final Portfolio Value","Total Return","Annualized Return","Volatility (Annual)","Sharpe Ratio","Maximum Drawdown","Number of Trades","Win Rate"),
  Value = c(paste0("ÂŁ", format(metrics$final_value, big.mark = ",", digits = 2, nsmall = 2)),
            paste0(sprintf("%.2f", metrics$total_return_pct), "%"),
            paste0(sprintf("%.2f", metrics$annualized_return_pct), "%"),
            paste0(sprintf("%.2f", metrics$volatility_pct), "%"),
            sprintf("%.3f", metrics$sharpe_ratio),
            paste0(sprintf("%.2f", metrics$max_drawdown_pct), "%"),
            as.character(metrics$number_of_trades),
            ifelse(is.na(metrics$win_rate_pct), "N/A", 
                   paste0(sprintf("%.2f", metrics$win_rate_pct), "%")))
)

kable(metrics_summary, 
      caption = "Detailed Performance Metrics - Prediction-Based Strategy",
      col.names = c("Metric", "Value"))
```

---

#Interpretation & Discussion

Performance Analysis:

```{r performance-analysis, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
pred_return <- results$comparison$Total_Return_Pct[results$comparison$Strategy == "Prediction-Based"]
bh_return <- results$comparison$Total_Return_Pct[results$comparison$Strategy == "Buy & Hold"]
random_return <- results$comparison$Total_Return_Pct[results$comparison$Strategy == "Random Trading"]

pred_sharpe <- results$comparison$Sharpe_Ratio[results$comparison$Strategy == "Prediction-Based"]
bh_sharpe <- results$comparison$Sharpe_Ratio[results$comparison$Strategy == "Buy & Hold"]

cat("Key Findings:")

if (pred_return > bh_return) {
  cat("he prediction-based strategy outperformed buy & hold by ", sprintf("%.2f", pred_return - bh_return), " percentage points.\n")
} else {
  cat("The prediction-based strategy underperformed buy & hold by ", sprintf("%.2f", bh_return - pred_return), " percentage points.\n")
}

if (pred_sharpe > bh_sharpe) {
  cat("Risk-adjusted performance (Sharpe ratio) is better than buy & hold (", sprintf("%.3f", pred_sharpe), " vs ", sprintf("%.3f", bh_sharpe), ").\n")
} else {
  cat("Risk-adjusted performance (Sharpe ratio) is worse than buy & hold (", sprintf("%.3f", pred_sharpe), " vs ", sprintf("%.3f", bh_sharpe), ").\n")
}

if (pred_return > random_return * 2) {
  cat("he strategy significantly outperforms random trading, suggesting genuine predictive skill.\n")
} else {
  cat("The strategy's performance is not significantly better than random trading**, which raises concerns about predictive power.\n")
}
```

# References
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0377221717310652
https://colah.github.io/posts/2015-08-Understanding-LSTMs/
https://karpathy.github.io/2015/05/21/rnn-effectiveness/
https://keras.io/getting_started/faq/
